title: Pets: reading poetry; Dog Language
description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using the poem Dog Language

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the poem. 

##Dog Language## 
 
by Marion Hovey Brigg

![A happy dog wagging its tail and licking its owner's face.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9017cc9fca3862e62a158ba9b970a183.jpg)

1. Our Towser is the finest dog that ever wore a collar,    
We wouldn't sell him—no, indeed—not even for a dollar!  
I understand his language now, 'cause honest, it appears  
That dogs can talk, and say a lot, with just their tails and ears.  

2. When I come home from school he meets me with a joyous bound,  
And shakes that long tail sideways, down and up, and round and round.  
Pa says he's going to hang a rug beside the door to see  
If Towser will not [[☃ definition 1]] it while he's busy greeting me.  

3. Then when he sees me get my hat, but thinks he cannot go,  
His ears get limp, his tail drops down, and he just walks off—slow;  
Though if I say the magic words: "Well, Towser, want to come?"  
Why, say! You'd know he answered "Yes," although at speech he's [[☃ definition 2]].  

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**What evidence from stanzas 1 and 2  best shows that the speaker cares for Towser?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: People often *beat* a rug by hitting a tool of some kind against the rug to get the dust out.
An old-fashioned term that meant "unable to speak". We no longer use the word *dumb* this way.

options.explanation:
NP: Non-Prose

##Dog Language## 
 
by Marion Hovey Brigg

![A happy dog wagging its tail and licking its owner's face.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9017cc9fca3862e62a158ba9b970a183.jpg)

1. Our Towser is the finest dog that ever wore a collar,    
We wouldn't sell him—no, indeed—not even for a dollar!

2. When I come home from school he meets me with a joyous bound,

3. Then when he sees me get my hat, but thinks he cannot go,

Though if I say the magic words: "Well, Towser, want to come?"  
Why, say! You'd know he answered "Yes," although at speech he's [[☃ definition 4]].

choices.content: Stanza 2 describes Towser’s feelings when the speaker comes home, and stanza 3 tells us how Towser feels when the speaker is leaving.
Stanza 2 shows what Towser looks like when he is sad, and stanza 3 explains what made Towser feel that way.
Stanza 2 explains how Towser behaves when he is excited, and stanza 3 shows how Towser makes his owner feel excited too.
Stanza 2 shows how Towser helps out around the house, and Stanza 3 shows how Towser’s owners feel about his help.

Stanza 1 introduces the idea that Towser is a talented dog, and stanza 2 shows how Towser uses his talents to help the speaker’s family.
Stanza 1 shows that Towser loves his owner, and stanza 2 gives examples about why Towser feels this way.
Stanza 1 explains what Towser’s barks mean, and Stanza 2 gives an example of a message Towser barks to his owners.

##Dog Language## 
 
by Marion Hovey Brigg

![A happy dog wagging its tail and licking its owner's face.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9017cc9fca3862e62a158ba9b970a183.jpg)

1. Our Towser is the finest dog that ever wore a collar,    
We wouldn't sell him—no, indeed—not even for a dollar!  
I understand his language now, 'cause honest, it appears  
That dogs can talk, and say a lot, with just their tails and ears.  

2. When I come home from school he meets me with a joyous bound,  
And shakes that long tail sideways, down and up, and round and round.  
Pa says he's going to hang a rug beside the door to see  
If Towser will not [[☃ definition 3]] it while he's busy greeting me.  

3. Then when he sees me get my hat, but thinks he cannot go,  
His ears get limp, his tail drops down, and he just walks off—slow;  
Though if I say the magic words: "Well, Towser, want to come?"  
Why, say! You'd know he answered "Yes," although at speech he's [[☃ definition 4]].  

[[☃ explanation 2]]

**How does Towser feel about his owner?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which line from the poem tells us how Towser feels about his owner?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

options.definition: People often *beat* a rug by hitting a tool of some kind against the rug to get the dust out.
An old-fashioned term that meant "unable to speak". We no longer use the word *dumb* this way.

choices.content: Towser loves his owner.
Towser is annoyed by his owner.
Towser is mad at his owner.
Towser acts like his owner.
“When I come home from school he meets me with a joyous bound,”
“His ears get limp, his tail drops down, and he just walks off—slow;”
“We wouldn't sell him—no, indeed—not even for a dollar!”
“That dogs can talk, and say a lot, with just their tails and ears.”
